card.title: The Blue Zones: Where People Live Longest
card.teaser: Five small corners of the planet have ten times more centenarians than average. What are they doing right?
card.coverImagePrompt: A weathered 100-year-old Sardinian shepherd smiling in golden hour light, olive groves rolling behind him, cinematic portrait, warm earthy tones

lessons.title: The island that forgot to die
lessons.body: In the mountains of Sardinia there's a village called Seulo where men reach 100 at ten times the global rate. On the Japanese island of Okinawa, women at 90 look after their great-grandkids. In Loma Linda, California, a community lives a decade longer than their American neighbors. Five tiny places on five different continents — all quietly beating the odds of aging.

visual.imagePrompt: A world map with five glowing blue circles across Sardinia, Okinawa, Nicoya, Ikaria, and Loma Linda, elegant cartography

lessons.title: What 'Blue Zones' means
lessons.body: Researcher Dan Buettner partnered with National Geographic to map pockets of exceptional longevity — places where people were measurably more likely to reach 100 in good health. On his first trip to Sardinia, a demographer drew blue circles on a map to mark the clusters. The name stuck. The five confirmed Blue Zones are Sardinia, Okinawa, Nicoya (Costa Rica), Ikaria (Greece), and Loma Linda (California).

keyTerms.definition: A person who has reached 100 years of age
A geographic region with unusually high rates of healthy centenarians

visual.imagePrompt: A vintage cartographer's desk with a paper map, blue pen circles, magnifying glass, warm library lighting

lessons.title: The Power 9
lessons.body: The Blue Zones are thousands of miles apart but share a startlingly similar way of life. People move naturally all day — walking, gardening, hauling firewood — rather than exercising. They eat mostly plants. They eat until they're 80% full. They have a clear sense of purpose (the Okinawans call it 'ikigai'). They belong to strong social networks. They drink moderately with friends, not alone. And crucially — they rarely retire from meaning.

visual.imagePrompt: Elderly people tending a hillside garden together under a clear Mediterranean sky, laughing, grounded earth tones

lessons.title: It isn't the genes
lessons.body: Danish twin studies suggest only about 20% of how long you live is genetic — the rest is lifestyle and environment. When Okinawans moved to Brazil and adopted a Western diet, their longevity advantage vanished within a generation. On Ikaria, even dementia rates are roughly 75% lower than in the U.S. The point isn't that these people won the genetic lottery. It's that their daily environment quietly makes the healthy choice the default.
lessons.funFact: The average Okinawan centenarian eats sweet potatoes as roughly 60% of their diet. Not fish, not seaweed — humble purple sweet potatoes.

visual.imagePrompt: A long wooden table outdoors with an extended family of all ages sharing a simple Mediterranean meal, wine, warm evening light

lessons.title: Build your own Blue Zone
lessons.body: You can't move to Sardinia tomorrow, but you can steal the design. Walk to the shop instead of driving. Eat dinner with the same friends twice a week. Plant something. Know why you get up in the morning. No single habit extends life dramatically — but stacked together, they tilt the odds by years. Longevity isn't a diet. It's a whole operating system.

quiz.question: Which of these is NOT one of the five Blue Zones?
quiz.explanation: Reykjavik isn't a Blue Zone. The five are Sardinia, Okinawa, Nicoya (Costa Rica), Ikaria, and Loma Linda (California).

quiz.question: Roughly what share of lifespan is genetic, according to twin studies?
quiz.explanation: Danish twin studies suggest around 20% of lifespan is inherited — the rest is shaped by lifestyle and environment.

quiz.question: What do most Blue Zone populations have in common?
options: Plant-heavy diets and strong social ties
quiz.explanation: Blue Zones cluster around mostly-plant diets, natural daily movement, strong community, and a sense of purpose.

sources.title: The Blue Zones: Lessons for Living Longer From the People Who've Lived the Longest
Genetic influence on human lifespan and longevity
